# AI Trends in Real-Time Analytics: What You Need to Know

![Photo of The Strategy Team](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.contentstack.io%2Fv3%2Fassets%2Fbltb564490bc5201f31%2Fblt40665f253c289e2d%2F67a3bcb04347300648996fe4%2FOrange_Profile_Picture_-_Brandmark_(1).png&w=750&q=75)

###### The Strategy Team

October 25, 2024

Share:

While you read this sentence, **generative AI systems** worldwide have analyzed millions of data points and made countless decisions—from predicting equipment failures in power plants to automatically adjusting supply chain logistics.  

This isn't future tech—it's happening right now. 

Perhaps [the biggest AI trend](https://www.microstrategy.com/research-and-reports/barc-data-bi-and-analytics-trend-monitor) in real-time analytics is its quick adoption. The gap between AI users and laggards widens daily, and the cost of waiting could devastate your bottom line. 

A [McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/McKinsey/Business%20Functions/Marketing%20and%20Sales/Our%20Insights/Five%20facts%20How%20customer%20analytics%20boosts%20corporate%20performance/Datamatics.pdf) study indicates that companies leveraging advanced data analytics are 23 times more likely to acquire customers. They are also 19 times more likely to profit than those who do not adopt artificial intelligence. 

The time to act is now.

## The Current State of Real-Time Analytics

**Real-time analytics** equips your organization with the insights needed for moment-by-moment decision-making.

What was once considered a technological luxury is now an essential business tool, with compelling results to prove its worth:

[75% of businesses](https://psico-smart.com/en/blogs/blog-the-impact-of-realtime-data-analytics-on-business-performance-metrics-167845#:~:text=Moreover%2C%20the%20evolution%20of%20real,in%20a%20rapidly%20evolving%20environment.) have invested in AI analytics, and [80% of these organizations](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/real-time-real-value-80-of-businesses-see-revenue-increases-thanks-to-real-time-data-301542344.html#:~:text=In%2DLanguage%20News-,Real%2Dtime%2C%20real%20value:%2080%25%20of%20businesses%20see,Deutschland%20%2D%20Deutsch) report direct revenue growth as a result.

Traditional analytics challenges centered around data latency, processing limitations, and storage constraints. Consequently, organizations struggled with outdated **batch analytics** processing systems, leading to delayed insights and missed opportunities. 

However, recent technological improvements in cloud-native architectures, stream processing engines, and in-memory computing have made **real-time analytics** more accessible and efficient. Though organizations still need to consider specific use cases and scalability needs, the reduced barrier to entry is far lower than before. With the technology more accessible than ever, organizations that delay adoption risk falling behind.

## The Benefits of Real-time Analytics for Decision-making

Every day, **artificial intelligence** tackles problems that once seemed insurmountable, creating breakthroughs that ripple across healthcare, science, and society. 

### Edge Computing and AI Integration

Edge computing directly moves **data processing** from a distant server in a **data warehouse** into where data is created (like devices, sensors, or machines), making everything faster and more efficient. This reduces latency, enabling split-second decision-making for applications like autonomous vehicles and smart manufacturing. 

Other real-world applications include predictive maintenance in industrial settings, smart traffic management systems, and IoT devices that make autonomous decisions without cloud connectivity. Such technological convergence is valuable when immediate responses are critical for safety or operational efficiency.

### AutoML in Real-Time Decision Making

**AI applications** democratize access to advanced analytics. Through continuous learning and adaptation, [AutoML systems](https://www.techtarget.com/searchenterpriseai/definition/automated-machine-learning-AutoML) automatically refine models based on new data, ensuring optimal performance over time. Companies like Netflix and Uber leverage AutoML to decide content recommendations and ride matching. 

Your business may use it for dynamic eCommerce pricing, which adjusts prices based on demand patterns, or for financial fraud detection systems that evolve to identify new fraud schemes. This technology makes sophisticated **analytics capabilities** accessible to businesses of all sizes. 

### Natural Language Processing for Instant Insights

Instead of navigating complicated queries, users can ask questions such as, "How did our sales perform last month?" and get instant answers. Voice-activated dashboards are bringing this convenience to mobile teams, while real-time text analysis helps businesses stay on top of customer feedback and social media conversations as they happen. 

By making **data analysis** more accessible to everyone—not just technical experts—NLP transforms how organizations make faster, smarter decisions. This shift from technical complexity to natural interaction means teams spend less time figuring out how to ask questions and more time acting on the answers.

## Challenges of Implementing Real-time Analytics in the Age of AI

Of course, successfully implementing AI data analytics requires organizations to carefully navigate several common challenges.

### Data Quality and Governance

Without question, data quality and privacy are primary concerns, requiring robust frameworks to guarantee data accuracy, consistency, and compliance with regulations. Organizations tend to struggle with fragmented **data sources**, incomplete records, and maintaining data privacy standards. 

Establishing clear data ownership roles, regular quality audits, and standardized metadata management can address fragmentation. Additionally, investing in modern data integration tools with baked-in compliance streamlines privacy management and data consistency.

### Technical Infrastructure

Often, legacy systems prove inadequate for AI workloads, necessitating substantial investments in cloud computing, storage solutions, and processing capabilities. However, with implementation [costs ranging between $6,000 and $300,000](https://www.uptech.team/blog/ai-cost), organizations find themselves caught between the need to modernize and budget constraints. 

Implementing AI in carefully planned stages rather than all at once can reduce costs. We recommend starting with critical workloads while maintaining legacy systems for less demanding operations. Also, many cloud services offer pay-as-you-go scalability, allowing you to spread costs over time.

### The Skills Gap

Another persistent challenge is the skills gap, with qualified AI professionals in high demand. So, businesses must develop comprehensive training programs while competing for talent in a tight market. Internal upskilling initiatives, strategic hiring, and partnerships with educational institutions can bridge this gap. 

In the immediate short term, creating cross-functional teams that blend technical business expertise helps spread AI knowledge throughout your organization, fostering a culture of continuous learning and innovation. As AI analytics become as common as email, a shared understanding will help your team adapt and thrive in an increasingly AI-driven workplace.

## Industry-Specific Applications of Real-time AI Analytics

Here’s **how AI is powering the future of real-time analytics** across diverse industries, each with unique applications:

### Healthcare

In healthcare, real-time patient monitoring systems use AI to analyze vital signs, predict potential complications, and alert medical staff before critical situations develop. These systems process thousands of data points per second, enabling early intervention and significantly improving patient outcomes.

![bi-healthcare.jpg](https://images.contentstack.io/v3/assets/bltb564490bc5201f31/blt2af7a6ed87988790/669929a1d316c7256ab344e5/bi-healthcare.jpg)

### Retail

In retail, AI drives hyper-personalization through real-time customer behavior analysis. Retailers leverage AI to customize shopping experiences, from dynamic pricing to personalized product recommendations, increasing customer engagement and sales conversion rates by [up to 30%](https://retalon.com/blog/hyper-personalization-in-retail).

![retail-knowledge-graphs.jpg](https://images.contentstack.io/v3/assets/bltb564490bc5201f31/blt8672b1fc8cdd3ccc/66aa315fadec83e0f72d1ac5/retail-knowledge-graphs.jpg)

### Financial

Financial services institutions employ **AI tools** for instantaneous risk assessment and trading decisions. Advanced algorithms analyze market trends, news sentiment, and transaction patterns in milliseconds, enabling automated fraud detection and more accurate investment strategies.

![the-role-of-bi-in-financial-compliance.jpg](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.contentstack.io%2Fv3%2Fassets%2Fbltb564490bc5201f31%2Fblt974bd94bca5657f6%2F6718b505a2400184faf4fbe8%2Fthe-role-of-bi-in-financial-compliance.jpg&w=3840&q=100)

### Governance

Smart city initiatives use real-time data to optimize traffic flow and emergency response times. [AI-powered predictive analytics](https://www.microstrategy.com/solutions/federal-government) help prevent fraud in social services and improve tax compliance. In public safety, AI systems analyze surveillance footage and sensor data to detect potential security threats, enabling faster response times and better resource allocation.

## Get Real-time Analytics to Empower Your Decision-making

No longer restricted to data scientists, AI-powered analytics now empowers everyone—from analysts reviewing dashboards to managers making quick decisions online—to instantly access and act on data insights. 

As we move forward, the companies that thrive will not just be those with the most data but those that can transform it into actionable insights at the speed of business. 

### MicroStrategy's AutoSuite: Democratizing and Accelerating AI-Driven Decisions

MicroStrategy's Auto suite exemplifies this shift. 

Features like Auto SQL and Auto Dashboard democratize data analysis, while Auto Answers and HyperIntelligence make complex insights accessible to users across all skill levels.

Introduction to Real-Time Data Processing
-----------------------------------------

### Overview of Real-Time Data Processing

In today's fast-paced business environment, real-time data processing has become essential for companies looking to stay competitive. Real-time data processing refers to the ability to process and analyze data as soon as it is generated, providing immediate insights that can drive operational efficiency and decision-making. Unlike traditional batch processing, which processes data in large chunks after it has been collected, real-time processing occurs almost instantaneously, allowing businesses to react to changes as they happen.

The benefits of real-time data processing are clear. Companies can improve customer experiences by responding to user behavior in real time, optimize supply chain operations by immediately addressing disruptions, and enhance fraud detection by identifying suspicious activity as it occurs. Examples of real-time data processing in action include recommendation engines that update based on user interactions, financial trading systems that adjust to market fluctuations, and IoT devices that monitor and respond to environmental conditions.

### The Importance of Real-Time Processing in Modern Business

The importance of real-time data processing in modern business cannot be overstated. In an era where data-driven decision-making is crucial, the ability to access and analyze data as it is generated gives companies a significant edge. Real-time processing enables businesses to make informed decisions faster, reducing the time between data generation and actionable insight.

For instance, in the e-commerce sector, real-time processing allows companies to adjust pricing dynamically based on demand, inventory levels, and competitor prices. In healthcare, it enables real-time monitoring of patient vitals, allowing for immediate interventions when necessary. The importance of real-time data processing extends across industries, driving innovation and improving operational efficiency in ways that were not possible with traditional data processing methods.

Core Components of Real-Time Data Processing
--------------------------------------------

### Data Ingestion and Processing

At the heart of real-time data processing is the ability to ingest and process data as it arrives. Data ingestion refers to the process of collecting and importing data from various sources, such as sensors, applications, or databases, into a processing system. In real-time environments, this data needs to be ingested and processed with minimal latency to ensure timely insights.

Data pipelines play a crucial role in this process, providing the infrastructure needed to move data from its source to the processing architecture. These pipelines are designed to handle large volumes of data, ensuring that the system can scale as data flows increase. Connectors are often used to integrate various data sources into the pipeline, facilitating seamless data flow.

The processing architecture must also support the rapid processing of incoming data, enabling real-time analytics and decision-making. This architecture typically includes components like stream processors, which process data streams in real-time, and data warehouses, where processed data is stored and made available for analysis.

### Stream Processing vs. Batch Processing

Real-time data processing is often contrasted with batch processing, which processes data in groups or batches after it has been collected. While batch processing is suitable for scenarios where real-time analysis is not critical, it lacks the immediacy required in many modern applications.

Stream processing, on the other hand, is designed for real-time environments. It processes data as it is generated, enabling immediate analysis and action. For example, in financial services, stream processing allows for real-time monitoring of transactions to detect fraud. In IoT applications, it enables the continuous monitoring of sensor data to trigger automated responses.

Latency is a critical factor in real-time processing, as it determines the delay between data generation and its availability for analysis. Low latency is essential for real-time systems, as it ensures that data is processed quickly enough to be useful. Processing systems designed for low latency often employ techniques such as in-memory processing and distributed computing to achieve the necessary speed.

### Scalability and Fault Tolerance

As businesses increasingly rely on real-time data processing, scalability and fault tolerance have become essential considerations. Scalability refers to the ability of a system to handle growing amounts of data and processing demands without degrading performance. In real-time environments, this means that the processing architecture must be able to scale up as data volumes increase, ensuring that insights remain timely and relevant.

Fault tolerance, on the other hand, refers to the system's ability to continue operating in the event of a failure. Real-time processing systems must be designed to handle failures gracefully, ensuring that data is not lost and that processing can resume quickly. This often involves the use of redundant components, such as multiple data streams or backup processors, to ensure that the system remains operational even in the face of hardware or software failures.

In addition to these considerations, processing systems must also be able to handle large volumes of data without compromising on performance. This requires efficient data ingestion, processing, and storage solutions that can scale with the business's needs.

Implementing Real-Time Data Processing
--------------------------------------

### Technologies and Tools

Implementing real-time data processing requires a combination of advanced technologies and tools that can handle the demands of real-time environments. [ETL tools](https://portable.io/learn/best-etl-tools) are commonly used to process data in real-time. Some of the most popular technologies used in real-time data processing include:

*   **Portable:** Portable offers no-code integrations that can stream data from upstream sources into your data warehouse - for instance syncing data in real-time from [Postgres to Snowflake](https://portable.io/learn/postgres-to-snowflake).
*   **AWS** (Amazon Web Services): AWS offers a range of cloud-based services that support real-time data processing, including data storage, stream processing, and machine learning.
*   **Apache Kafka**: Kafka is a distributed streaming platform that allows for the real-time ingestion and processing of data streams. It is widely used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.
*   **Spark**: Apache Spark is a unified analytics engine that supports both batch and stream processing. It is known for its speed and scalability, making it a popular choice for real-time data processing.
*   **Apache Flink**: Flink is another powerful stream processing framework that supports real-time analytics. It is designed for low-latency, high-throughput data processing, making it ideal for real-time applications.
*   **SQL**: Structured Query Language (SQL) remains a fundamental tool for querying and managing data in real-time processing systems. It is often used in conjunction with other technologies to provide real-time analytics.

These technologies, often open-source, provide the backbone for real-time data processing systems, enabling businesses to implement real-time analytics and decision-making at scale. Data integration is also a critical component, as it ensures that data from various sources can be seamlessly combined and processed in real-time.

### Real-Time Analytics and Decision-Making

Real-time analytics is the process of analyzing data as it is generated, allowing businesses to make informed decisions quickly. This is particularly important in scenarios where immediate action is required, such as detecting fraud, responding to customer interactions, or managing supply chain disruptions.

Near real-time processing refers to situations where data is processed with a slight delay, typically measured in seconds or milliseconds. While not as immediate as true real-time processing, near real-time analytics can still provide valuable insights in a timely manner, making it suitable for many applications.

Big data plays a significant role in real-time analytics, as it involves processing and analyzing large volumes of data from various sources. Real-time dashboards are often used to visualize this data, providing a clear and immediate view of key metrics and trends. These dashboards allow decision-makers to monitor performance in real-time, enabling them to respond quickly to changing conditions.

### Real-Time Data Warehousing

Data warehouses have traditionally been used for storing and analyzing large volumes of historical data. However, with the advent of real-time data processing, data warehouses are increasingly being used to support real-time analytics as well.

Real-time data warehousing involves the continuous ingestion of data into the warehouse, where it is immediately available for querying and analysis. This requires processing systems that can handle the high throughput and low latency demands of real-time environments. ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes, which have traditionally been used to prepare data for analysis, must also be adapted for real-time processing, ensuring that data is ingested and transformed as it is generated.

In real-time data warehousing, data streams play a crucial role, providing the continuous flow of data needed for real-time analysis. This allows businesses to gain insights from both historical and real-time data, providing a comprehensive view of their operations.

Use Cases and Applications
--------------------------

### Industry-Specific Use Cases

Real-time data processing has a wide range of applications across various industries. Some of the most notable use cases include:

*   **E-Commerce**: Real-time data processing allows e-commerce platforms to personalize customer experiences by analyzing user behavior as it happens. This includes recommending products based on browsing history, adjusting prices based on demand, and optimizing inventory management.
*   **IoT** (Internet of Things): IoT devices generate vast amounts of data in real time, which can be used for monitoring, automation, and decision-making. For example, in smart homes, real-time processing can be used to adjust lighting, heating, and security systems based on user preferences and environmental conditions.
*   **Healthcare**: In healthcare, real-time data processing enables the continuous monitoring of patient vitals, allowing for immediate interventions when necessary. This can improve patient outcomes and reduce the risk of complications.
*   **Supply Chain**: Real-time data processing allows for the dynamic management of supply chains, enabling businesses to respond quickly to disruptions, optimize inventory levels, and improve delivery times.
*   **Fraud Detection**: Real-time analytics is essential for detecting fraudulent activity as it occurs. This is particularly important in financial services, where the ability to identify and respond to fraud in real time can prevent significant losses.

### Leveraging Real-Time Data Processing for Business Intelligence

Business intelligence (BI) is another area where real-time data processing is making a significant impact. By analyzing data as it is generated, businesses can gain immediate insights that drive strategic decision-making. This is particularly important in competitive industries, where the ability to act on data quickly can provide a significant advantage.

Pricing strategies, for example, can be optimized in real time based on factors such as competitor pricing, inventory levels, and customer demand. Providers of real-time data processing services often offer tools that enable businesses to automate these decisions, ensuring that pricing remains competitive and aligned with market conditions.

Machine learning is also being integrated into real-time data processing systems, allowing businesses to predict trends and make proactive decisions. For example, machine learning algorithms can analyze real-time data to identify patterns and anomalies, enabling businesses to detect potential issues before they escalate.

These examples highlight the versatility and effectiveness of real-time data processing across various industries.

### Real-Time Data Processing in Modern Applications

In the current digital landscape, apps play a significant role in driving business growth, particularly through the integration of real-time data processing capabilities. Whether it’s social media platforms or e-commerce applications, the ability to process and analyze data as it is generated allows these apps to deliver real-time insights that enhance user experiences and operational efficiency.

Data sets used in these applications are often vast and varied, requiring robust data analysis techniques to extract meaningful insights. Data analytics tools are employed to process these data sets, enabling businesses to make data-driven decisions quickly. For instance, social media platforms leverage real-time data analytics to tailor content recommendations, enhance user engagement, and optimize ad targeting.

Formats of data processed in real-time applications can vary widely, from structured data in relational databases to unstructured data from social media feeds. APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are crucial in this context, as they facilitate the seamless integration of different data sources and processing systems, enabling real-time data exchange and analysis.

Despite the rise of real-time processing, batch data processing remains relevant, particularly in scenarios where large volumes of data need to be processed periodically rather than continuously. Data engineering plays a critical role in managing both real-time and batch processing workflows, ensuring that data is efficiently ingested, processed, and stored for subsequent analysis.

Incorporating real-time insights into app development and data engineering practices is essential for staying competitive in today’s fast-paced market. By combining real-time and batch processing, businesses can create more responsive and data-driven applications that meet the needs of their users.

Best Practices and Future Trends
--------------------------------

### Optimizing and Automating Real-Time Data Processing

To maximize the benefits of real-time data processing, businesses should focus on optimization and automation. This involves fine-tuning processing systems to reduce latency, improve scalability, and ensure fault tolerance. Automated workflows can help streamline processes, reducing the need for manual intervention and ensuring that data is processed efficiently.

Operational efficiency is another key consideration. By optimizing real-time data processing systems, businesses can reduce costs, improve performance, and enhance the overall user experience. This often involves leveraging cloud-based services, such as those offered by AWS, to scale processing capacity as needed.

### Visualization and Immediate Insights

Visualization tools play a crucial role in real-time data processing, enabling businesses to gain immediate insights from their data. Dashboards, for example, provide a real-time view of key metrics, allowing decision-makers to monitor performance and respond to changes as they happen.

Immediate insights are particularly valuable in dynamic environments, where conditions can change rapidly. By visualizing data in real time, businesses can identify trends, detect anomalies, and make informed decisions quickly.

Relational databases and data lakes are often used to store and manage the data that feeds these visualizations. By combining real-time data with historical data, businesses can gain a comprehensive view of their operations, enabling them to make more informed decisions.

### Emerging Trends and Future Outlook

As real-time data processing continues to evolve, several emerging trends are shaping the future of the industry:

*   **Edge Computing:** Edge computing is becoming increasingly important for real-time data processing, particularly in IoT applications. By processing data closer to its source, edge computing reduces latency and improves the speed of decision-making.
*   **AI and Machine Learning**: AI and machine learning are being integrated into real-time data processing systems, enabling businesses to predict trends, automate decisions, and enhance their overall analytics capabilities.
*   **5G Technology:** The rollout of 5G networks is expected to drive significant advancements in real-time data processing. With faster data transmission speeds and lower latency, 5G will enable new use cases and improve the performance of existing real-time processing systems.
*   **Data Management**: As data volumes continue to grow, effective data management will become increasingly important. This includes ensuring data quality, managing data lakes, and optimizing processing architectures to handle large-scale real-time data processing.

In conclusion, real-time data processing is transforming the way businesses operate, enabling them to make faster, more informed decisions. By understanding the core components, implementing the right technologies, and following best practices, companies can harness the power of real-time data processing to drive growth and stay ahead of the competition.

The future of data analytics is unfolding faster than most organizations can keep pace with. As we move through 2025, the convergence of artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and real-time processing capabilities is creating unprecedented opportunities for businesses to extract value from their data. But with these opportunities come new challenges and considerations that will fundamentally reshape how we approach analytics.

The data analytics field has transformed far from its roots in basic reporting and historical analysis. Today’s organizations face a radically different environment where data streams continuously, decisions happen in milliseconds, and the line between technical and business roles continues to blur. The future of data analytics isn’t just about processing more information faster. It’s about fundamentally rethinking how we create, share, and monetize insights across the enterprise.

What makes this moment particularly exciting is the democratization of advanced analytics capabilities. Technologies that were once the exclusive domain of tech giants are now accessible to organizations of all sizes. From real-time streaming platforms to composable architectures, from ethical AI frameworks to cross-functional collaboration tools, the building blocks for next-generation analytics are within reach.

In this article, we’ll explore ten critical trends that are shaping the future of data analytics. These aren’t distant possibilities but developments already transforming how leading organizations operate today. Whether you’re a data engineer, business analyst, or executive, knowing these trends will be essential for thriving in the rapidly changing analytics environment and positioning your organization for success in an increasingly data-driven world.

1. The increasing velocity of big data analytics
------------------------------------------------

The days of exporting data weekly, or monthly, then sitting down to analyze it are long gone. In the future, big data analytics will increasingly focus on [data freshness](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-freshness-explained/) with the ultimate goal of real-time analysis, enabling better-informed decisions and increased competitiveness.

[Streaming data](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-batch-processing-vs-stream-processing/), as opposed to processing it in batches, is essential for gaining real-time insight, but has implications when it comes to maintaining data quality – fresher data can mean a higher risk of acting on inaccurate or incomplete data (which can be be addressed using the principles of [data observability](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-what-is-data-observability/)).

![Image 23](blob:http://localhost/b6d3df199499efb691fafb281a172c89)

Materialized tables were described as the mid-point between streams & tasks and materialized views

[Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/), for example, announced Snowpipe streaming at this year’s summit. The company has refactored their Kafka connector and made it so that when data lands in Snowflake it is queryable immediately resulting in a 10x lower latency.

Google recently announced that [PubSub](https://www.google.com/search?client=safari&rls=en&q=PubSub&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8) can directly stream into BigQuery as well as the launch of [Dataflow Prime](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/guides/enable-dataflow-prime), an upgraded version of their managed streaming analytics service.

On the data lake side, [Databricks](https://www.databricks.com/) has launched [Unity Catalog](https://databricks.com/product/unity-catalog) to help bring more metadata, structure, and governance to data assets.

### Real-time data/insights

Being able to access real-time data for analysis might sound like overkill to some, but that’s just no longer the case. Imagine trading Bitcoin based on what it was worth last week or writing your tweets based on what was trending a month ago.

Real-time insight has already shaken up industries like finance and social media, but its implications beyond them are huge: Walmart, for example, [has built](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/blazing-trail-cloud-computing-how-walmart-built-one-/) what may be the world’s largest hybrid cloud to, among other things, manage their supply chains and analyze sales in real time.

### Real-time, automated decision making

Machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) are already being successfully employed in industries like healthcare, for detection and diagnosis, and manufacturing, where intelligent systems track wear and tear on parts. When a part is close to failure, the system might automatically reroute the assembly line elsewhere until it can be fixed.

That’s a practical example, but there are all sorts of applications beyond this: email marketing software that can diagnose the winner of an A/B test and apply it to other emails, for example, or analysis of customer data to determine loan eligibility. Of course, businesses that don’t yet feel comfortable **fully** automating decisions can always retain a final step of manual approval.

2. The heightened veracity of big data analytics
------------------------------------------------

The more data we collect, the more difficult it is to ensure it’s accuracy and quality. To read more about this, check out our recent post on the future of [data management](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-management/), but for now, let’s get into trends surrounding the veracity of big data analytics.

### Data quality

Making data-driven decisions is always a sensible business move…unless those decisions are based on bad data. And data that’s incomplete, invalid, inaccurate, or fails to take context into account is bad data. Fortunately, many data analytics tools are now capable of identifying and drawing attention to data that seems out of place.

It’s always best, of course, to diagnose a problem rather than treating the symptom. Instead of just relying on tools to identify bad data in the dashboard, businesses need to be scrutinizing their pipelines from end to end. Figuring out the right source(s) to draw data from for a given use case, how it’s analyzed, who is using it, and so on, will result in healthier data overall and should reduce issues of data downtime.

### Data observability

There’s more to observability than just monitoring and alerting you to broken pipelines,. An understanding of the five [pillars of data observability](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-what-is-data-observability/) – freshness, schema, volume, distribution, and lineage – is the first step for businesses looking to get a handle on the health of their data and improve its overall quality.

![Image 25](blob:http://localhost/377d514f9820a06fc3ea0fbcdccdca6d)

Data observability platforms like Monte Carlo alert data teams to schema, volume, freshness, and distribution anomalies.

Beyond that, a [data observability platform](https://www.montecarlodata.com/product/data-observability-platform/) like Monte Carlo can automate monitoring, alerting, lineage, and triaging to highlight data quality and discoverability issues (and potential issues). The ultimate goal here is to eliminate bad data altogether and prevent it from recurring.

### Data governance

With the volumes of data we’re talking about here, taking proper protective measures becomes even more important. Compliance with measures like the [General Data Protection Regulation](https://www.databricks.com/)(GDPR) and [California Consumer Privacy Act](https://oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa) (CCPA) is vital to avoid fines, but there’s also the issue of how damaging data breaches can be to a company’s brand and reputation.

We’ve previously written about [data discovery](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-catalogs-are-dead-long-live-data-discovery/) – real-time insights about data across domains, while abiding by a central set of governance standards – but it’s worth bringing up again here.

Creating and implementing a [data certification program](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-stop-treating-your-data-engineer-like-a-data-catalog-and-how-to-build-a-data-certification-program/) is one way to ensure that all departments within a business work only using data that conforms to appropriate and agreed upon standards. Beyond that, [data catalogs](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-observability-first-data-catalog-second-heres-why/) can be used to outline how stakeholders can (and can’t) use data.

3. Storage and analytics platforms are handling larger volumes of data
----------------------------------------------------------------------

By using cloud technology, things like storage availability and processing power can be [virtually infinite](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-the-future-of-the-data-engineer/). Businesses no longer need to worry about buying physical storage or extra machines, because they can use the cloud to scale to whatever level they need at that moment.

Beyond that, cloud data processing means that multiple stakeholders can access the same data at the same time without experiencing slowdown or roadblocks. It also means that, as long as the right security measures are in place, up to the minute data can be accessed at any time and from anywhere.

The [current status quo](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-lake-vs-data-warehouse/) for this is [data warehousing](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-the-future-of-data-warehousing/), with most notable providers – Snowflake, [Redshift](https://aws.amazon.com/redshift/), [BigQuery](https://aws.amazon.com/redshift/) – operating in the cloud. Elsewhere, Databricks and their “data lakehouse” combine elements of [data warehouses and data lakes](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-warehouse-vs-data-lake-vs-data-lakehouse-definitions-similarities-and-differences/).

But the primary aim remains the same: data, analysis, and potentially AI, in one (or just a few) places. Of course, more data also means a pressing need for more/better ways to handle, organize, and display these large data sets in a way that’s easily digestible.

Keenly aware of that need, modern business intelligence tools ([Tableau](https://www.tableau.com/), [Domo](https://www.domo.com/), [Zoho Analytics](https://www.zoho.com/analytics/), etc.) are increasingly prioritizing the importance of dashboarding to more easily manage and track large volumes of information to enable data-driven decisions.

4. Processing data variety is easier
------------------------------------

With larger volumes of data comes, typically, more disparate sources of data. Managing all these different formats, along with obtaining any sort of consistency, is virtually impossible to do manually…unless you have a very large team that’s fond of thankless tasks.

Tools like [Fivetran](https://www.fivetran.com/) come equipped with 160+ data source connectors, from marketing analytics to finance and ops analytics. Data can be pulled from hundreds of sources, and prebuilt (or custom) transformations applied, to create reliable data pipelines.

Similarly, Snowflake has partnered with services like [Qubole](https://www.qubole.com/) (a cloud big data-as-a-service company) to build ML and AI capabilities into their data platform: with the right training data, ‘X’ data being imported will result in ‘Y’ happening within Snowflake.

Fortunately, the emphasis in big data analytics is currently very much on finding ways to collate data from different sources and find ways to use it together rather than trying to force consistency before data is loaded where it needs to be.

5. Democratization and decentralization of data
-----------------------------------------------

For many years, business analysts and executives have had to turn to in-house data scientists when they needed to extract and analyze data. Things are very different in 2022, with services and tools that enable non-technical audiences to engage with data.

We’re seeing more emphasis on [analytics engineering](https://www.thoughtspot.com/data-trends/data-and-analytics-engineering/analytics-engineer-vs-data-engineer), with tools like dbt focused on “modeling data in a way that empower end users to answer their own questions.” In other words, enabling stakeholders rather than analyzing or modeling projections for them.

Plus, there’s lots of talk about a more visual approach – modern business intelligence tools like Tableau, [Mode](https://mode.com/), and [Looker](https://www.looker.com/) all talk about visual exploration, dashboards, and best practices on their websites. The movement to democratize data is well and truly underway.

### No-code solutions

As their name suggests, no-code tools rework an existing process to take away any coding knowledge that may previously have been required. On the consumer side we’ve seen products like Squarespace and Webflow do exactly that, but tools like [Obviously AI](https://www.obviously.ai/) are shaking up the big data analytics space in a similar way.

The biggest advantage of no-code (and low code) tools is that they enable stakeholders to get to grips with data without having to pester the data team. This not only frees up data scientists to work on more intensive activities, but also encourages data-driven decisions throughout the company because engaging with data is something that everyone is now capable of.

### Microservices / data marketplaces

The use of microservices architecture breaks down monolithic applications into smaller, independently deployable services. In addition to simplifying deployment of these services, it also makes it easier to extract relevant information from them. This data can be remixed and reassembled to generate or map out different scenarios as needed.

That can also be useful for identifying a gap (or gaps) in the data you’re trying to work with. Once you’ve done that, you can use a data marketplace to fill in those gaps, or augment the information you’ve already collected, so you can get back to making data-driven decisions.

### Data mesh

The aim of using a [data mesh](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-what-is-a-data-mesh-and-how-not-to-mesh-it-up/) is to break down a monolithic data lake, decentralizing core components into distributed data products that can be owned independently by cross-functional teams.

By empowering these teams to maintain and analyze their own data, they get control over information relevant to their area of the business. Data is no longer the exclusive property of one specific team, but something that everyone contributes value to.

6. Leveraging GenAI and RAG
---------------------------

We’re entering a transformative era in big data analytics as two emerging trends – generative AI (GenAI) and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) – gain traction.

[GenAI](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-generative-ai-data-engineering/) is particularly exciting. It pushes the boundaries of traditional data analysis, allowing us to generate synthetic datasets and automate content creation. This innovation opens up new avenues for predictive analytics and data visualization, which were previously limited by the scale and scope of manually gathered datasets. As data engineers, our role is evolving from merely managing data flows to actively participating in the generation of data that can provide deeper insights and foster innovation in various business domains.

[RAG](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-the-moat-for-enterprise-ai-is-rag-fine-tuning/), on the other hand, presents a unique challenge and opportunity. It enhances the AI models by augmenting them with real-time data retrieval, ensuring that the insights generated are not only accurate but also contextually relevant. The integration of RAG into our data systems requires a sophisticated understanding of how to efficiently orchestrate data flows and ensure the seamless retrieval of relevant information. This necessitates an advanced skill set in data pipeline architecture, focusing on agility and accuracy, to support the dynamic nature of RAG-enabled systems.

7. The rise of data products and analytics monetization
-------------------------------------------------------

The shift from viewing analytics as a cost center to a revenue generator is fundamentally changing how organizations approach their data strategies. Forward-thinking companies are now packaging their analytics outputs as distinct “[data products](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-data-products-101/)” complete with dedicated [data product managers](https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-what-good-data-product-managers-do-and-why-you-probably-need-one/), service-level agreements (SLAs), and clear value propositions.

Data Analytics Technology Trends 2025
-------------------------------------

Data analytics in 2024 is driven by AI, machine learning, NLP, data mesh, edge computing, and cloud technologies. These innovations enable faster processing, improved insights, and wider data accessibility. Let’s take a closer look at these trends.

**Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning**

Businesses can now automate an even wider range of data processing tasks, from anomaly detection to predictive maintenance. As of 2025, [nearly 65%](https://kanerika.com/blogs/data-analytics-trends-2025/) of organizations have adopted or are actively investigating AI technologies for data and analytics.Thanks to artificial intelligence technology, AI and ML-powered forecasting has become increasingly sophisticated, allowing organizations to anticipate market trends and user behavior with remarkable accuracy.

**Natural Language Processing**

From sentiment analysis of customer feedback to content summarization and classification, NLP plays a pivotal role in data analysis. The technology widens the range of data sources, informing strategic decisions with a richer, more nuanced understanding of market trends and customer needs.

**Data Mesh**

By decentralizing data ownership and governance, data mesh enables cross-functional teams to easily access, share, and derive insights from their data assets. This has led to improved data-driven collaboration and the extraction of greater value from corporate data.

**Edge Computing**

By processing data closer to its source, edge solutions minimize latency and bandwidth usage, making them ideal for real-time analytics in IoT, industrial, and other time-sensitive environments. This has enabled organizations to detect anomalies, predict maintenance needs, and make rapid, informed decisions based on sensor data.

**Data-as-a-Service**

DaaS platforms allow businesses to outsource data storage, processing, and predictive modeling, empowering them to leverage enterprise-grade tools and expertise without the need for significant infrastructure investments.This trend has opened the door for smaller companies to compete on a more level playing field.

**Data Democratization**

Leading enterprises have prioritized data democratization to foster a more collaborative, data-literate culture. By making data and analytics tools available to a wider range of decision-makers, organizations are breaking down silos, improving cross-functional alignment, and accelerating time-to-insight.

**Big Data Integration**

Organizations increasingly invest in advanced analytics solutions, powered by Big Data. From predictive maintenance in manufacturing to hyper-personalized marketing in retail, data-driven decision-making has become essential for maintaining a competitive edge in today’s fast-paced, data-rich business environment.

**Cloud Computing**

Cloud-based platforms offer improved accessibility, scalability, and cost-efficiency, empowering organizations to rapidly scale their data processing and storage capabilities to meet evolving business demands. Companies now focus on extracting insights from their data using cloud-native applications rather than managing complex on-premises infrastructure.

Agentic AI

The rise of AI systems capable of autonomous decision-making is poised to change workflows and boost forecast accuracy across industries. Traditional AI models passively analyze data and await human input, while agentic AI operates with a higher degree of autonomy. Such systems set goals, plan tasks, execute actions, and adapt based on feedback without continuous human oversight. By 2028, it's projected that [33% of enterprise software](https://www.blueprism.com/resources/blog/ai-agentic-agents-survey-statistics/) applications will incorporate agentic AI, a significant increase from less than 1% in 2024.

Data Analytics in the Healthcare Industry
-----------------------------------------

By 2025, the integration of [AI services](https://www.coherentsolutions.com/artificial-intelligence) and machine learning into healthcare analytics is enhancing predictive capabilities, automating complex tasks, and providing deeper insights into patient care.

In 2024, more than 70% of healthcare institutions[use cloud computing to facilitate real-time data sharing and collaboration](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/2024/03/22/rethinking-healthcare-tech-and-data-analytics-strategies-in-2024/).

Cloud-native data analytics platforms help them predict trends, personalize treatments, enhance patient outcomes, improve operational efficiency, and streamline operations.

**AI-Powered Diagnostics**

AI algorithms are enhancing diagnostic accuracy across various medical fields. Machine learning models can now analyze medical imaging with superhuman precision, detecting subtle abnormalities in X-rays, MRIs, and CT scans. This technology is accelerating diagnosis, reducing human error, and enabling earlier intervention for conditions like cancer and heart disease.

**Predictive Analytics**

Healthcare providers are increasingly using predictive models to anticipate patient needs and outcomes. These tools analyze historical data to forecast disease progression, identify high-risk patients, and optimize resource allocation. Predictive analytics is proving particularly valuable in managing chronic diseases and preventing hospital readmissions, leading to improved patient care and reduced healthcare costs.

**Value-Based Care and Population Health Management**

Data analytics is driving the shift towards value-based care models, focusing on patient outcomes rather than service volume. By analyzing population health data, healthcare organizations can identify trends, target interventions, and allocate resources more effectively. This approach improves overall community health while optimizing healthcare spending and quality of care.

**Personalized Medicine and Precision Healthcare**

Analytics is enabling tailored treatment plans based on an individual’s genetic makeup, lifestyle, and environmental factors. By analyzing vast datasets of genetic information and treatment outcomes, healthcare providers can predict drug efficacy and potential side effects for specific patients, leading to more effective treatments and improved patient outcomes.

**Internet of Medical Things and Real-Time Data Processing**

The proliferation of connected medical devices is generating unprecedented amounts of real-time patient data. Advanced analytics platforms can process this data stream, providing immediate insights to healthcare professionals. This enables continuous patient monitoring, early detection of health issues, and rapid response to critical situations, enhancing both in-hospital care and remote patient management.

**Data Architecture and Governance**

Healthcare organizations are prioritizing robust data architecture and governance to manage the growing volume of health data securely and efficiently. This includes implementing interoperable systems, ensuring data quality, and maintaining patient privacy. Strong data governance enables seamless data sharing across healthcare networks, facilitating collaborative research and improving continuity of care.

Data Analytics Trends in Accounting
-----------------------------------

According to Xerox’s[Accounting & Bookkeeping ‘State of the Industry’](https://www.xero.com/us/campaign/accounting-industry-report/)Report for 2023, 66% of businesses in the accounting service industry reported improved staff retention, increased revenue, and higher profits—all thanks to cloud accounting software.

Data analytics enhances the ways accountants analyze data, predict trends, and source strategic insights. Accounting firms and experts use this technology to elevate their operational efficiency and make more informed financial decisions.

**AI Automation in Accounting**

AI-powered automation is streamlining routine accounting tasks, from data entry to reconciliation. Machine learning algorithms can process vast amounts of financial data, identify patterns, and flag anomalies with unprecedented speed and accuracy. This not only reduces human error but also frees up accountants to focus on higher-value activities like financial strategy and advisory services.

**Cloud Accounting**

Cloud-based accounting solutions enable real-time collaboration, remote access, and seamless integration with other business systems. This shift to the cloud enhances data security, scalability, and accessibility, allowing accountants to work more efficiently and provide timely insights. Cloud platforms also facilitate easier compliance with evolving regulations and standards.

**Advanced Data Analytics Tools**

Sophisticated analytics tools are empowering accountants to extract meaningful insights from complex financial datasets. These tools enable predictive modeling, scenario analysis, and data visualization, enhancing financial forecasting and risk management. By leveraging advanced analytics, accountants can provide more strategic guidance to businesses, driving informed decision-making and value creation.

**Business Intelligence in Accounting**

Business intelligence tools are transforming how financial data is analyzed and presented. Interactive dashboards and visual analytics enable accountants to communicate complex financial information more effectively to stakeholders. This trend is enhancing financial reporting, performance monitoring, and strategic planning, allowing businesses to respond more quickly to market changes.

The Future of Data Analytics in Banking and Finance
---------------------------------------------------

McKinsey reports that banks and finance institutions that implement advanced analytics workbenches in 2024[witnessed their corporate and commercial revenues rise](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-data-and-analytics-edge-in-corporate-and-commercial-banking)by more than 20% over three years.

Analytics allow finance experts to tailor products to individual needs, reduce fraud incidents by analyzing transaction patterns, and improve loan approval processes through predictive modeling. And that is just the tip of the iceberg.

**Investment in Data Initiatives**

Financial institutions are significantly increasing their investments in data analytics initiatives. This trend reflects the growing recognition of data’s strategic value in driving business growth and operational excellence. Banks are allocating resources to build robust data infrastructures, develop advanced analytics capabilities, and cultivate data-driven cultures across their organizations.

**Market Growth**

The big data analytics market in banking is experiencing rapid expansion, driven by the increasing adoption of digital technologies and the growing volume of financial data. This growth is fueled by the demand for real-time analytics, personalized financial services, and improved risk management capabilities. As the market evolves, we’re seeing a rise in specialized analytics solutions tailored for specific banking functions.

**Advanced Data Analytics in Banking**

Banks are leveraging advanced analytics techniques to gain a competitive edge in corporate and commercial banking. These tools enable more accurate credit risk assessment, personalized product recommendations, and optimized pricing strategies. By harnessing the power of machine learning and AI, banks can provide tailored financial solutions and improve customer retention in increasingly competitive markets.

**Big Data Analytics and Risk Management**

Data analytics is transforming risk management in banking, enabling more sophisticated approaches to identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks. Advanced analytics models can process vast amounts of structured and unstructured data to detect fraud patterns, assess creditworthiness, and predict market volatility. This data-driven approach enhances regulatory compliance and strengthens overall financial stability.

Banks are focusing on integrating data analytics capabilities across their entire technology stack. This involves modernizing legacy systems, adopting cloud-based platforms, and implementing advanced data management tools. By creating a unified data ecosystem, banks can break down silos, improve data quality, and enable more agile and responsive decision-making processes throughout the organization.

Data Analytics Trends in Insurance

Insurers are increasingly integrating diverse data sources, from telematics to social media, to gain a holistic view of their customers and operations. As insurers increasingly rely on AI-powered decision-making, there is a growing emphasis on ensuring these systems are transparent, unbiased, and accountable. Human Resources Trends

Why invest in data analysis for your SaaS?
------------------------------------------

SaaS organizations already sit on a goldmine of data. Analyzing the raw data can help you make concrete decisions regarding product development, [user onboarding](https://userpilot.com/blog/user-onboarding/), and more. It’s particularly helpful in the following cases:

*   **Optimizing performance:** Slow load times and frequent crashes can frustrate users. Real-time analytics helps identify and fix these performance issues before they drive users away.
*   **Enhancing customer experiences:** Analyzing [behavioral data](https://userpilot.com/blog/users-behavior/) helps you understand (and even anticipate) user needs, preferences, and pain points. This business intelligence helps you delight users with personalized guidance and interactions, ultimately improving the user experience.
*   **Driving strategic decisions:** Still relying on guesswork to scale your business? Use predictive analytics to identify hidden patterns and trends and make smarter, data-backed decisions for growth.

15 Trends in data analytics shaping SaaS businesses
---------------------------------------------------

Advancements in generative AI, zero-trust security, and other technologies guide how SaaS companies collect and process data, making insights more accessible, secure, and actionable.

Let’s explore the key trends shaping the future of SaaS data analytics.

### 1. Generative AI-powered predictive analytics

According to a [Gartner survey](https://www.gartner.com/peer-community/oneminuteinsights/omi-unleashing-power-generative-ai-data-analytics-jjq), 40% of business leaders are already using Gen AI in their data and analytics (D&A) functions.

Predictive analytics, on the other hand, helps businesses forecast future outcomes based on historical data. Gen AI models can help simulate complex data scenarios and minimize forecasting errors. That, in turn, allows SaaS organizations to [predict customer behavior](https://userpilot.com/blog/predictive-customer-analytics/) and market trends accurately.

For instance, AI-powered CRM platforms can preempt churn and implement data-backed retention strategies. Similarly, financial planning tools can simulate economic conditions to guide investment decisions.

### 2. Embedded analytics

Modern SaaS solutions integrate a data analytics layer within the application. These tools can collect, store, and process data without third-party [analytics tools](https://userpilot.com/blog/customer-analytics-platforms/).

Embedded analytics offers several benefits, including access to real-time insights and improved data visualization and reporting. With up-to-date metrics always at your fingertips, you can make swift and efficient decisions regarding [user engagement](https://userpilot.com/blog/user-engagement-metrics/), product adoption, and more.

### 3. Collaborative analytics platforms

The growing adoption of hybrid work is also driving the demand for collaboration-friendly analytics tools. Businesses want data analytics solutions that let cross-functional teams collaborate on data projects with ease.

With collaborative analytics solutions, data sharing is a breeze. You can share insights, dashboards, and reports in real time, minimizing the risk of data inconsistencies. They also promote transparency and speed up decision-making.

For instance, Userpilot lets you create [custom dashboards](https://userpilot.com/blog/analytics-dashboard-examples/) to track and analyze user data. Any team member can access these dashboards to pull up relevant metrics and reports.

### 4. Enhanced data security and regulatory compliance

SaaS data breaches affect [more than 30%](https://appomni.com/reports/state-of-saas-security/) of organizations worldwide. These incidents have far-reaching legal and financial consequences. Plus, they take a toll on brand reputation and customer trust.

The widespread adoption of data analytics in SaaS emphasizes the need for advanced compliance and security measures.

Techniques like data encryptions and role-based access controls help protect sensitive user details. They’re also useful for adhering to data privacy regulations, such as GDPR, CCPA, and [HIPAA](https://userpilot.com/blog/hipaa-compliant-analytics-tools/). AI-powered threat detection and compliance automation can come in handy, too.

### 5. Zero trust analytics

We can’t talk about data security and not mention zero trust as one of the most important data analytics trends. A [Gartner survey](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2024-04-22-gartner-survey-reveals-63-percent-of-organizations-worldwide-have-implemented-a-zero-trust-strategy) reports that 63% of organizations have fully or partly deployed a zero-trust strategy.

Perimeter-based security measures, such as firewalls and VPNs, are based on the concept of implicit trust. Zero-trust security, on the other hand, is based on the principle of **“never trust, always verify.”**

![Image 55: Zero-Trust-Components](blob:http://localhost/e8a57f4972516a1d3218894eabc89576)

Components of Zero Trust Architecture.

It relies on the continuous verification of user identities and access permissions to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data. Additionally, continuous threat monitoring helps detect insider threats and cyberattacks in real time and ensures timely mitigation.

### 6. Synthetic data generation

Analytics tools are only as good as the data available to them. How do you make the most of data analytics without jeopardizing sensitive information? The answer is synthetic data.

Generative AI models are being used to create synthetic data that mimics real-world scenarios but doesn’t contain actual user details. The artificial data is then used to train machine learning models, test SaaS applications, and analyze product usage.

Synthetic data generation provides a cost-effective and scalable way to leverage analytics while protecting user privacy. It isn’t surprising that by 2026, [75% of businesses](https://www.gartner.com/en/articles/3-bold-and-actionable-predictions-for-the-future-of-genai) are expected to create synthetic customer data using Gen AI. That’s a significant jump from less than 5% in 2023.

### 7. Personalized customer experiences

A one-size-fits-all customer experience isn’t enough to motivate business users to renew their subscriptions.

Modern consumers don’t want to be treated like statistics. [81%](https://hyken.com/research/) expect hyper-personalized experiences that speak to their unique needs and pain points.

Analyzing customer behavior data and feedback can help you tailor interactions, content, and offerings for each individual. It’s the key to improving user engagement, satisfaction, and retention.

Plus, with personalized product recommendations and [contextual guidance](https://userpilot.com/blog/contextual-help/), you can tap into new upselling opportunities. For instance, Userpilot lets you identify power users and target the segment with personalized offers:

### 8. Self-service analytics

Traditional data analytics involves a fair share of coding and is, therefore, out of reach for non-technical users. Marketing, sales, and customer success teams have to depend on IT for data analysis and reporting, which is inefficient and time-consuming.

As SaaS companies become more dependent on data, one of the other data analytics trends that are growing in popularity is the need for [self-service analytics](https://userpilot.com/blog/self-serve-analytics/). With intuitive interfaces and drag-and-drop functionality, self-service analytics tools let users visualize data and generate custom reports with a few clicks.

Non-technical teams no longer have to wait for tech support to analyze their processes and tell them what’s working and what isn’t. Faster access to [data-driven insights](https://userpilot.com/blog/marketing-data-insights/) speeds up decision-making, too.

### 9. Augmented analytics

You perform retention analysis and find that [user retention](https://userpilot.com/blog/new-user-retention/) rates have increased by 20% since the previous year. Did it happen because you invested in personalization? Or was it due to the new features you launched? Or did the revamped in-app help center work its magic?

Augmented analytics helps you read between the lines and understand what your data is trying to tell you. It uses ML and NLP to automate data processing, discovery, and sharing and makes data analysis accessible to non-technical users.

With augmented analytics, you can use queries in natural language to create reports with relevant metrics or generate predictions. The increasing dependence on augmented analytics is evident from its exponential market growth, which is projected to [cross $173 billion by 2032](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2024/01/08/2805836/0/en/Augmented-Analytics-Market-to-Grow-at-CAGR-of-27-6-through-2032-Growing-Need-for-Customer-Centric-Analytics.html).

### 10. Advanced data visualization

Visualization plays a major role in data science and its options are no longer restricted to graphs and charts. For complex datasets, you need immersive visualization tools that use augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) interfaces.

Advanced visualization techniques, such as 3D plots, [heatmaps](https://userpilot.com/blog/heat-map-analysis/), and interactive dashboards, improve data comprehension and help spot trends accurately. Userpilot simplifies complex data analysis through intuitive dashboards and easily understandable data sets. For instance, you can use [funnel path analysis](https://userpilot.com/blog/funnel-analysis-examples/) to identify precise factors that drive users away.

### 11. Automated data governance

Data governance outlines parameters and policies for maintaining data quality, accuracy, and privacy. However, enforcing these standards can be difficult in the chaos of managing vast datasets.

A 2024 survey revealed that [45% of UK and US firms](https://itbrief.co.uk/story/report-reveals-95-of-uk-us-firms-struggle-with-data-issues) considered their organization’s data governance to be ineffective. Additionally, 60% of organizations also reported that their governance policies didn’t align with business strategies, leading to outdated data and inefficient decision-making.

Automated governance is the solution to these challenges. It uses AI and ML to enforce data standards, track data quality, and monitor compliance in real time. It minimizes errors due to human oversight and improves data reliability.

### 12. Natural language processing (NLP) in data analytics

Modern analytics tools allow you to interact with data through simple, natural language queries. That, in turn, makes data exploration accessible to all stakeholders, regardless of technical prowess.

Instead of relying on complex SQL queries, you can simply ask questions like, “What were our best-performing features in the last quarter?” and retrieve relevant insights.

NLP-driven analytics can be crucial to improving the adoption of [data-driven strategies](https://userpilot.com/blog/data-driven-insights/) in cross-functional teams within SaaS companies.

### 13. Edge computing for real-time data processing tasks

Let’s say you want to deliver a targeted onboarding experience to each user based on their responses to a welcome survey. If you wait for the survey responses to reach a remote server where they’re analyzed, you can’t personalize the experience in real time. That, in turn, can frustrate users and increase churn.

Edge computing is the key to minimizing these delays and getting your hands on real-time insights. It processes data closer to its source, reducing latency and bandwidth usage. According to an [Accenture survey](https://www.accenture.com/in-en/insights/cloud/edge-computing), 65% of companies already used edge computing in 2023. Another 83% also believed that it would give them a competitive edge in the future.

It’s particularly crucial for SaaS applications that depend on immediate decision-making to improve the user experience.

### 14. Metadata-driven data fabric

Organizations managing vast amounts of data need a streamlined approach to integrate, govern, and analyze it. Metadata-driven data fabric automates data management by using metadata to classify, connect, and optimize data flows.

The use of metadata streamlines data integration and helps identify data opportunities and issues. Plus, it minimizes the need for manual intervention and accelerates project times.

For instance, if your customer behavior data is spread across multiple platforms, you can use a metadata-driven data fabric to automatically identify redundant datasets, unify fragmented records, and surface quality issues.

### 15. Data literacy and training programs

Even the most feature-rich data analytics tools won’t offer much value when your employees can’t use them. As SaaS companies become more dependent on data-driven workflows, improving data literacy within your organization is a must.

Unfortunately, only [35% of businesses](https://www.computerweekly.com/news/366616280/IT-and-business-leaders-face-big-gap-in-understanding-of-data-priorities) have a strong data culture, with inconsistent support and education being the prime reason for failure.

Invest in training programs and AI-assisted tools to enhance your team’s ability to understand and leverage data effectively. Help them understand how to use data-backed decisions in their daily tasks. Finally, build a culture of continuous learning so employees can adapt to the fast-changing landscape of data analytics.

enriched_points.expanded_content: As businesses strive for competitive advantage, real-time analytics enables immediate decision-making by processing data as it arrives, enhancing operational efficiency and customer experiences.

enriched_points.point_title: Advancements in AI and Machine Learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI and machine learning are transforming analytics by automating insights, improving predictive capabilities, and facilitating personalized experiences, thus driving smarter business decisions and innovations.
Edge computing processes data closer to its source, reducing latency and bandwidth usage, which is crucial for real-time applications like IoT, enabling swift and informed decision-making.
